steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Mora and colleagues' specific claim is that clicks and chirps in chemically defended caterpillars primarily warn (educate) predators rather than startle them. A supporting finding must show that clicks and chirps are especially suited to teaching, not just stopping, predators.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Short-duration sounds like clicks and chirps tend to be especially effective in training animals to associate a behavior with a consequence.: This directly supports the education mechanism — if short-duration sounds are best for conditioning, that explains why clicks and chirps function as warnings rather than startle signals. ✓
  • Choice B — Caterpillars that use chemical defenses produced sounds at a higher volume than did caterpillars without chemical defenses.: Volume is about startling, not educating. This wouldn't distinguish warning from frightening. ✗
  • Choice C — The acoustic characteristics of clicks and chirps make them particularly audible to bats, lizards, and birds, some of the most frequent predators of caterpillars.: Audibility explains reach, not the education vs. startle distinction the claim rests on. ✗
  • Choice D — Chickens and yellow warblers, two predators of caterpillars, have been observed to stop their attacks in response to caterpillar sounds of any duration.: “Any duration” undercuts the significance of short-duration clicks and chirps specifically, and stopping attacks could reflect startling rather than learning. ✗
That matches Choice A — Short-duration sounds like clicks and chirps tend to be especially effective in training animals to associate a behavior with a consequence..
